phenomenon: selective memory strengthening through encoding orientation
claim: Processing information for survival relevance produces superior retention compared to other encoding strategies including imagery, pleasantness, self-reference, and intentional learning.

phenomenon: sources of subjective well-being in material possession
claim: Happiness is jointly determined by both having desired items and desiring possessed items, with each pathway uniquely mediated by different psychological antecedents.

phenomenon: belief in agency and ethical behavior regulation
claim: Belief in free will is causally necessary for moral behavior; exposure to deterministic messaging reduces adherence to ethical norms.

phenomenon: perceptual memory constraints for auditory versus visual input
claim: Auditory change detection operates via different mechanisms than visual change detection, showing memory effects independent of stimulus complexity rather than complexity-dependent.

phenomenon: physical distance priming and affective/cognitive judgment shifts
claim: Perceptual and motor representations of spatial distance directly influence emotional and evaluative judgments independent of self-referential distance construal.

phenomenon: functional trade-offs in emotion regulation goal-pursuit
claim: People regulate emotions not only for hedonic pleasure but also instrumentally, selecting unpleasant emotions when situationally useful despite short-term affective costs.

phenomenon: social disconnection compensation through humanization
claim: Lonely individuals increase humanization of nonhuman agents and belief in anthropomorphized entities as a direct compensatory response to social deficit.

phenomenon: group-level cooperation versus intergroup competition motivation
claim: Individual self-sacrifice in intergroup conflict is predominantly motivated by altruistic in-group cooperation rather than aggressive out-group harm.

phenomenon: linguistic word-order constraints on real-time phonological activation
claim: Language-specific word-order rules operate on-line to modulate which phonological representations become active during speech production.

phenomenon: female mate-preference modulation by fertility status and genetic quality trade-off
claim: Women's preference for attached versus single male partners reverses across the menstrual cycle, balancing paternal investment at low fertility against genetic quality access at peak conception risk.

phenomenon: visual affordance and action semantics in spatial search guidance
claim: Dorsal-stream action-based representations of object affordance and prototypicality guide visual search attention, with stronger effects in the lower visual field.

phenomenon: moral judgment modulation via physical purity associations
claim: Activating or inducing physical cleanliness reduces moral severity through intuitive processes, reversing disgust-driven moral harshness via embodied purity-morality associations.

phenomenon: edge geometry cues for depth and figure-ground perception
claim: Extremal edges (viewpoint-specific self-occlusion horizons) operate as potent depth and figure-ground cues, stronger than classical proximity and convexity cues.

phenomenon: methodological artifacts in cross-national personality trait measurement
claim: Self-reported and peer-reported national personality averages fail to capture between-nation personality differences due to reference-group effects; only perceptions of national character correlate with behavioral predictors.

phenomenon: personality dominance and spatial-attentional metaphor processing
claim: Dominant individuals preferentially allocate spatial attention to the vertical dimension due to metaphoric conceptualization of dominance as vertical hierarchy.

phenomenon: goal-shielding under response conflict during multitask management
claim: Response conflicts trigger immediate goal-shielding that reduces susceptibility to interference and distraction on the conflict trial itself.

phenomenon: rapid threat detection of evolutionarily relevant stimuli in early development
claim: Young children exhibit enhanced rapid detection of snakes over other stimuli, paralleling adult patterns and suggesting innate attentional bias to ancestral threats.

phenomenon: semantic spatial associations and perceptual simulation in attention
claim: Object words activate perceptual simulations of objects' typical spatial locations, orienting attention to those locations and interfering with target identification there.

phenomenon: causal attribution through counterfactual mental simulation
claim: People assess causality by simulating counterfactual alternatives; events are judged more causal when their absence undoes the outcome.

phenomenon: working memory compression via learned statistical regularities
claim: Working memory capacity remains constant but remembered items increase when learners discover and exploit statistical redundancies in input to create compressed representations.
